title: The Warmth of Nothing in Particular
showNotes:
The outpost was quiet in the way that only happens when everyone is pretending to be busy. With no active roster to speak of — no names on the duty board, no boots by the door in the wrong order, no argument about who ate whose rations — the common room settled into a strange, expectant hush. The fire crackled. Snow tapped at the shutters with the patient persistence of someone who has nowhere better to be. A mug of something hot sat on the table, cooling slowly, as if waiting for a hand that hadn't arrived yet.

Outside, the wind moved through Frosthaven like it owned the place, which, in most practical senses, it did. It rattled the sign above the smithy and lifted a scarf from someone's fence post and carried it off northward toward the ice without ceremony or apology. A townsfolk woman watched it go, shrugged, and went back inside. There was wisdom in that. The kind of wisdom that only comes from surviving enough winters to stop being surprised by them.

The empty chair by the hearth rocked once — just slightly — as a draft found its way through a gap in the boards. Whoever had sat there last had left a mark: a faint impression in the worn cushion, a boot-scrape on the stone floor, the ghost of a presence that hadn't yet been replaced. Frosthaven had a way of holding onto people like that, even after they'd gone. Especially after they'd gone. It kept the shape of them a little longer than was strictly necessary, like a blanket that still holds warmth after the sleeper has risen. The next chapter hadn't written itself yet. But the fire was lit, the chairs were empty, and the cold had a way of filling a place with anticipation whether anyone asked it to or not.

title: The Soup Incident of No One's Fault (Officially)
showNotes:
The common room of the outpost smelled like ambition and regret in roughly equal measure. Someone — and the debate over who would outlast the winter itself — had decided that a proper hot meal was in order, and had furthermore decided that they were the person to make it. The pot had been bubbling for the better part of two hours. It had started as a vegetable broth. It had, through a series of confident decisions, become something else entirely: thick and grey-brown, with a surface that moved in ways that seemed only loosely connected to the heat beneath it. A spoon stood upright in the center without assistance, which several people noted aloud and one person called "a good sign, actually."

The tasting went around the table with the grim ceremony of a military tribunal. Faces were made. Assessments were offered in the careful diplomatic language of people who still had to sleep in the same building as the cook. "Hearty," someone said. "It's got... layers," offered another, in the tone of a person identifying structural damage. Outside, the wind pressed hard against the shutters and the aurora painted the sky in colors that had no names, indifferent and enormous and beautiful, as it always was. Inside, someone quietly fed their bowl to the fire when the cook's back was turned, and the fire, to its credit, did not complain.

title: What the Ice Remembers
showNotes: Date: March 25, 2026 The aurora was still bleeding across the sky when they broke camp — greens and silvers chasing each other through the dark — and every one of them remembered the Vermling's warning. *Careful when you see the aurora.* They moved faster for it, boots punching through the crust of old snow, the painting folded into an oilskin pouch against Owbert's chest. The valley found them before midday: a great curving bowl of stone and ice, the cliffs rising on all sides like a hand slowly closing into a fist. It matched the primitive map well enough. It matched it the way a grave matches a face — recognizably, and without comfort. The cave mouth was wide and dark and did not try to hide what it was. Inside, the lantern light fell across paintings older than anyone alive — demons rendered not as warnings but as neighbors, as kin, towering figures that shrank across the walls as the murals deepened until they were shown planting crops beside their human counterparts, stacking stone. The party moved through them in a silence that had less to do with caution and more to do with the particular hush that falls over people confronting something they had not been prepared to feel. Pyro's flame danced. Nightengale's eyes tracked the shapes above. And then the cold things came — drawn by warmth, by the exhalation of living breath in dead air — and the silence ended. Three rounds of violence erupted in the cramped dark, Riptide holding the passage while Owbert moved behind the lines in some frantic internal negotiation between self-preservation and curiosity. It was Eric who ended it in the third round with a ferocity that emptied the tunnel of anything breathing that shouldn't have been — three bodies falling in the span of a single terrible assault, the echo of it ringing off ice-coated stone long after the blows had landed. Four enemies fell in all. It was not enough. The cave went deeper than their strength could follow, and the fragments they had come for remained buried under ice and dark and the slow pressure of centuries. They retreated into the cold with nothing but experience and the knowledge of what lay waiting for them below. Some doors, the frozen north has always known, do not open the first time you knock. Frosthaven received them without ceremony, as it received all failures — with fires already burning, because the fires did not care about outcomes. The outpost was as they had left it: enduring, indifferent, alive in the minimal way that settlements on the edge of the world are alive. They came back carrying the memory of those murals like a splinter worked too deep to simply pull free — demons building alongside people, a history that refused the shape they had always given it. Owbert set the oilskin pouch on the table in the great hall and stared at it for a long time. Outside, the aurora had faded, but they all knew it would return. The Vermlings would be ready, they'd been told. The cave would be waiting too, patient in the way that ice is patient — which is to say, absolutely, and without any particular hurry.
